title_en: Structure: Ten Volumes from the Companions to the Sufis
title_ar: البنية: عشرة أجزاء من الصحابة إلى الصوفية
content_en:
Hilyat al-Awliya is organized in generational layers (tabaqat), beginning with the Prophet's companions and moving forward through successive generations of pious Muslims to Abu Nu'aym's own era. The work covers approximately 700 figures across these generations, devoting to each a collection of biographical notices, spiritual maxims, anecdotes illustrating their character and practice, and hadith they transmitted. This combination of biographical, spiritual, and hadith material is distinctive and reflects Abu Nu'aym's dual formation as a hadith scholar and Sufi sympathizer.

The first volume covers the companions, presenting them not primarily as legal transmitters — as companion biographical works like Al-Isti'ab do — but as spiritual exemplars. The material emphasized is their asceticism, their devotion in prayer, their detachment from worldly things, and their nearness to God. This spiritual emphasis on the companions reflects the Sufi tradition's claim that the companions' way of life embodied the ideal of Islamic spirituality and that the Sufi path is essentially a continuation and elaboration of the companions' approach to inner religious life.

Subsequent volumes cover the Successors (Tabi'in) and the generation after them — figures like al-Hasan al-Basri, Ibn Sirin, Sufyan al-Thawri, Ibrahim al-Nakha'i, and Malik ibn Anas — presenting them through the same spiritual lens. The material for these figures is particularly rich: Abu Nu'aym had access to substantial biographical and hadith material about the first and second centuries AH, and the figures he covers in these volumes were themselves major authorities in the Islamic scholarly tradition.

The later volumes cover the recognized masters of the Sufi tradition: al-Harith al-Muhasibi (the psychologist of the soul), Dhu al-Nun al-Misri (the Egyptian mystic), Bayazid al-Bistami (known for ecstatic utterances), Junayd al-Baghdadi (the 'sober' master of classical Sufism), al-Hallaj (the controversial martyr of love), and many others. Abu Nu'aym's inclusion of al-Hallaj, whose execution for blasphemy was controversial, within a work celebrating Islamic holiness was itself a statement of sympathetic engagement with the more radical Sufi tradition.
title_id: Struktur: Sepuluh Jilid dari Para Sahabat hingga Para Sufi
content_id:
Hilyat al-Awliya' disusun dalam lapisan-lapisan generasional (tabaqat), dimulai dari para sahabat Nabi dan bergerak maju melalui generasi-generasi berikutnya dari kaum Muslim yang saleh hingga era Abu Nu'aym sendiri. Karya ini mencakup sekitar 700 tokoh lintas generasi-generasi tersebut, mengabdikan untuk masing-masing sebuah kumpulan catatan biografis, maksim-maksim spiritual, anekdot-anekdot yang menggambarkan karakter dan praktik mereka, serta hadis-hadis yang mereka transmisikan. Kombinasi antara material biografis, spiritual, dan hadis ini bersifat khas dan mencerminkan pembentukan ganda Abu Nu'aym sebagai ulama hadis dan penganut tasawuf.

Jilid pertama mencakup para sahabat, menyajikan mereka bukan terutama sebagai perawi hukum — sebagaimana dilakukan karya-karya biografi sahabat seperti Al-Isti'ab — melainkan sebagai teladan-teladan spiritual. Material yang ditekankan adalah kezuhudan mereka, kekhusyukan mereka dalam shalat, ketidakterlekatan mereka dari hal-hal duniawi, dan kedekatan mereka dengan Allah. Penekanan spiritual atas para sahabat ini mencerminkan klaim tradisi tasawuf bahwa cara hidup para sahabat mewujudkan ideal spiritualitas Islam dan bahwa jalan tasawuf pada dasarnya merupakan kelanjutan dan elaborasi dari pendekatan para sahabat terhadap kehidupan keagamaan batin.

Jilid-jilid berikutnya mencakup para Tabi'in dan generasi setelah mereka — tokoh-tokoh seperti al-Hasan al-Bashri, Ibn Sirin, Sufyan al-Tsawri, Ibrahim al-Nakha'i, dan Malik ibn Anas — menyajikan mereka melalui lensa spiritual yang sama. Material untuk tokoh-tokoh ini sangat kaya: Abu Nu'aym memiliki akses ke material biografis dan hadis yang substansial tentang abad pertama dan kedua H, dan tokoh-tokoh yang ia bahas dalam jilid-jilid ini sendiri merupakan otoritas-otoritas utama dalam tradisi keilmuan Islam.

Jilid-jilid kemudian mencakup para master yang diakui dalam tradisi tasawuf: al-Harits al-Muhasibi (psikolog jiwa), Dhu al-Nun al-Mishri (sufi Mesir), Bayazid al-Bisthami (dikenal karena ucapan-ucapan ekstatik), Junayd al-Baghdadi (master 'sadar' tasawuf klasik), al-Hallaj (martir kontroversial cinta), dan banyak lainnya. Penyertaan Abu Nu'aym atas al-Hallaj, yang eksekusinya karena penistaan agama bersifat kontroversial, ke dalam sebuah karya yang merayakan kesucian Islam sendiri merupakan pernyataan keterlibatan yang simpatik dengan tradisi tasawuf yang lebih radikal.
content_ar:
Hilyat al-Awliya is organized in generational layers (tabaqat), beginning with النبي ﷺ's الصحابة and moving forward through successive generations of pious Muslims to Abu Nu'aym's own era. The work covers approximately 700 figures across these generations, devoting to each a collection of biographical notices, spiritual maxims, anecdotes illustrating their character and practice, and الحديث they transmitted. This combination of biographical, spiritual, and الحديث material is distinctive and reflects Abu Nu'aym's dual formation as a الحديث scholar and Sufi sympathizer.

The first volume covers the الصحابة, presenting them not primarily as legal transmitters — as companion biographical works like Al-Isti'ab do — but as spiritual exemplars. The material emphasized is their asceticism, their devotion in prayer, their detachment from worldly things, and their nearness to God. This spiritual emphasis on the الصحابة reflects the Sufi tradition's claim that the الصحابة' way of life embodied the ideal of Islamic spirituality and that the Sufi path is essentially a continuation and elaboration of the الصحابة' approach to inner religious life.

Subsequent volumes cover the Successors (Tabi'in) and the generation after them — figures like al-Hasan al-Basri, Ibn Sirin, Sufyan al-Thawri, Ibrahim al-Nakha'i, and Malik ibn Anas — presenting them through the same spiritual lens. The material for these figures is particularly rich: Abu Nu'aym had access to substantial biographical and الحديث material about the first and second centuries AH, and the figures he covers in these volumes were themselves major authorities in the Islamic scholarly tradition.

The later volumes cover the recognized masters of the Sufi tradition: al-Harith al-Muhasibi (the psychologist of the soul), Dhu al-Nun al-Misri (the Egyptian mystic), Bayazid al-Bistami (known for ecstatic utterances), Junayd al-Baghdadi (the 'sober' master of classical Sufism), al-Hallaj (the controversial martyr of love), and many others. Abu Nu'aym's inclusion of al-Hallaj, whose execution for blasphemy was controversial, within a work celebrating Islamic holiness was itself a statement of sympathetic engagement with the more radical Sufi tradition.
